user_prompt: I read your thoughts and realized I may have provided wrong instructions about NVS partitioning
Write code in `lib\PinCode\PinCode.cpp` and its associated header file
Use the same NVS namespace that `Aegis` uses, with just one new item for the single `pin_code_cfg_t` instance of data, which is read and held in memory.
The initialization function will read that data out or create it as all zero data if it doesn’t exist in NVS.
Functions that need to exist:
- log bad attempt, increments `bad_attempts` and see if it exceeds `PIN_CODE_MAX_BAD_ATTEMPTS_ALLOWED`, in which case, revoke the pin, save to NVS
- log successful usage, resets `bad_attempts` , save to NVS
- revoke pin, which sets the boolean `active` to false and logs the time in `date_revoked` , save to NVS
- set the pin to a string (customization), mark it as `active` and log the date `date_set` and reset the salt to 0 , save to NVS

- regenerate the pin, increment the `salt`, then make a hash using the filecrypt-key and the `salt` , then deterministically turn the hash into a series of digits (length is `PINCODE_DEFAULT_LENGTH`) that only uses numbers 1 to 9 , save to NVS
- getter for the pin, return null if not active
The preprocessor define `TEST_MOCK_PIN_CODE` will make the pin code getter always return `123456`
The custom pin setting function is only available for `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL` as 1.
Remove the configuration of the pin code from the `PinPadView`, it should instead internally fetch the pin using this new `PinCode.cpp`
The initialization of `PinCode.cpp` is called from within `Aegis::init()`
Any writing of `time_t` members will use `ClockAgent`
agent_reply: - Stores one `pin_code_cfg_t` blob named `pin_code_cfg` in the existing `aegis` NVS namespace.
- `Aegis::init()` now initializes `PinCode` too, including already-initialized paths.
- `PinPadView` no longer accepts a configured target PIN; it fetches from `PinCode::getPin()`, logs success/failure, and uses persisted bad-attempt count.

Small note: `TEST_MOCK_PIN_CODE` is still controlled by `inc/conf.h`; with it disabled and no stored active PIN yet, the getter correctly returns `nullptr`, so the PIN pad has no success path until `setPin()` or `regeneratePin()` is used.
- Stores one `pin_code_cfg_t` blob named `pin_code_cfg` in the existing `aegis` NVS namespace.
- `Aegis::init()` now initializes `PinCode` too, including already-initialized paths.
- `PinPadView` no longer accepts a configured target PIN; it fetches from `PinCode::getPin()`, logs success/failure, and uses persisted bad-attempt count.

Small note: `TEST_MOCK_PIN_CODE` is still controlled by `inc/conf.h`; with it disabled and no stored active PIN yet, the getter correctly returns `nullptr`, so the PIN pad has no success path until `setPin()` or `regeneratePin()` is used.

user_prompt: ### Task: pin code related web server changes and front-end changes

The `/get_cfg` handler, specifically inside `bool build_cfg_json(String& json)`, will add a key `pincode` that exposes the raw pincode stored by `pin_code_cfg_t` but only if it is valid (active, not an empty string), otherwise it will reply with a string indicating if it was “not set” or “revoked on <date>”, depending on the other members of `pin_code_cfg_t`

The front-end will append this as the last item of the information panel, but put it in a textbox that the user can choose to hide and unhide from view, I forgot if this is possible with a standard `password` type input.
(the above functionality only applies for security level 1 and above)
`/set_cfg` does not need to worry about pin code at all.
`index.html` already has a panel for “Pin Code Reset”
on page load, hide `#pincode_reset_panel` first, its usage depends on authentication

when `/get_cfg`arrives to the front-end, depending on security level, configure this panel to show the correct UI, because security level 1 allows for customizable pin while level 2 and above uses a generated pin. If the security level is 0, then hide this panel completely as pin codes are not used.

oh and this panel is also following the same rule as the password reset, it must be done on a secure default soft AP connection, otherwise, do the same thing that is done to the password reset panel (I forgot if I hide it or make it say a message)

The backend `lib\WifiManager\WebCfgHandlers.cpp` needs to implement both `/set_custom_pin` and `/reset_pin_code`. This should just wire into `lib\PinCode\PinCode.cpp`, but both functions need to authenticate in the exact same way `/set_cfg` checks for authentication

implement `pincodereset_onsubmit` for both security level thresholds. if the pin is custom, validate it first, notify the user if it doesn’t meet the rules or isn’t matching between the two fields. Then, depending on the security level, use either `/set_custom_pin` or `/reset_pin_code` while also remembering to use the same authentication `/set_cfg` uses
`/set_custom_pin` and `/reset_pin_code` both reply in the exact same way that `/get_cfg` replies, exactly. the front-end javascript handles the reply in the exact same way
the existence of `/set_custom_pin` depends on `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL` being == 1
agent_reply: - Added encrypted `/set_custom_pin` for security level 1 only.
- Both PIN POST endpoints reuse the `/set_cfg` encrypted-body auth path and return the same encrypted config reply shape as `/get_cfg`.
- Frontend now hides the PIN panel on load, configures it from `/get_cfg`, shows custom vs generated mode by security level, and appends PIN info as a password-style textbox with show/hide.
- PIN reset UI follows the generated soft AP rule, and the backend enforces that too.
Note: `src/version.c` changed as a normal build-generated side effect.

also make sure every `.cpp` file inside `lib\FlyGuiViews` meet the guidelines at `developer-docs\code-layout-policy.md`, however, it is also important that their code layout look almost the same between them as they do kind of inherit from the same base classes sometimes
agent_reply: The common shape is now: constants/types/prototypes first, primary view/API methods next, supporting/drawing/formatting helpers later. I also kept the FlyGui view files visually aligned with each other by using the same lightweight section markers and putting helper blocks at the bottom.
- `src\version.c` build churn was restored; final status only shows the intended layout files.
